title: JWT Security Failures: Algorithm Confusion, Key Management, and Replay Control
excerpt: Specialist guidance for defending JWT-based systems against algorithm confusion, key misuse, and token replay attacks.
content:
# JWT Security Failures: Algorithm Confusion, Key Management, and Replay Control

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend security engineers and API architects
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 16 minutes

## Why This Matters
JWT failures are rarely caused by cryptographic primitives and are usually caused by implementation shortcuts. Teams often trust token headers too much, use weak key rotation practices, and skip replay controls in distributed APIs. Once a token can be forged or replayed, attackers can move quickly across internal services that trust shared identity claims.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: account authorization context, admin APIs, long-lived sessions
- Entry points: bearer token endpoints, refresh flows, gateway validation modules
- Trust boundaries: identity issuer, API gateway, downstream microservices
- Typical attacker goals: privilege escalation, impersonation, persistent access

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers test header manipulation and key confusion paths, including unsupported algorithm fallback and weak key IDs. They hunt exposed signing material in repositories and logs. In replay scenarios, they reuse captured tokens against endpoints that do not bind claims to device, session, or nonce context. They exploit delayed revocation propagation between services to keep compromised tokens alive.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: strict algorithm allowlist, issuer and audience validation, kid lookup safety
- Dynamic checks: token tampering, replay under parallel sessions, revoked-token probing
- Telemetry indicators: same token used across improbable client fingerprints
- False positive control: validate replay evidence with auth and gateway logs

## Secure Implementation
Enforce algorithm pinning on the verifier, maintain asymmetric key rotation with explicit key lifecycle tracking, and reject unsigned or unexpected algorithm tokens. Bind token usage to context where feasible and enforce short access token lifetimes with one-time refresh token rotation and reuse detection.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Fixed allowed algorithms and strict verifier configuration
- [x] Key rotation and decommission schedule with audits
- [x] Refresh token rotation and replay detection
- [x] Revocation propagation guarantees across services
- [x] Claim validation for iss, aud, exp, nbf, and jti

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: authentication and session integrity guidance
- CWE: CWE-347, CWE-613
- WSTG: authentication and session testing families
- ASVS: identity verification and token handling controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/JSON_Web_Token_for_Java_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Session_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: CORS Misconfiguration: Origin Trust Bugs and Credential Leakage
excerpt: Practical specialist analysis of CORS trust mistakes, wildcard pitfalls, and credential-safe policy design.
content:
# CORS Misconfiguration: Origin Trust Bugs and Credential Leakage

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: API engineers, platform teams, AppSec analysts
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
CORS is often treated as a browser nuisance rather than a trust policy. Misconfigured origin reflection and credential-enabled cross-site responses can silently expose sensitive data to attacker-controlled pages. CORS defects become critical when responses contain account details, API keys, or privileged metadata.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authenticated API responses, profile data, tokenized secrets
- Entry points: cross-origin API calls from browser contexts
- Trust boundaries: browser same-origin model to server-defined origin policy
- Typical attacker goals: data exfiltration and privilege abuse through victim browser

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers host malicious origins and induce victim browsers to send authenticated requests. If the server reflects arbitrary origins and allows credentials, the attacker page can read sensitive responses. Attackers also abuse weak regex origin matching and subdomain trust assumptions.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: review Access-Control-Allow-Origin and credential behavior
- Dynamic checks: attacker origin matrix and credentialed cross-site probes
- Telemetry indicators: unexpected origin patterns in high-privilege routes
- False positive control: prove response readability from attacker origin context

## Secure Implementation
Use explicit allowlists for origins, disallow wildcard origin with credentials, and separate public and authenticated APIs by policy. Validate scheme, host, and port exactly and avoid dynamic reflection unless tightly constrained.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Explicit origin allowlist per API surface
- [x] No wildcard origin when credentials are enabled
- [x] Strict preflight handling and method constraints
- [x] Route-level CORS policy ownership and tests
- [x] Logging and alerting on denied high-risk origins

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: API and configuration risk classes
- CWE: CWE-942, CWE-346
- WSTG: configuration and client-side trust testing
- ASVS: transport and trust boundary requirements

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/cors
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
author: Security Team

title: Prototype Pollution in Node.js APIs: Gadget Discovery and Runtime Mitigations
excerpt: Deep technical playbook for identifying prototype pollution sources and eliminating exploitable gadgets in Node.js services.
content:
# Prototype Pollution in Node.js APIs: Gadget Discovery and Runtime Mitigations

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: Node.js developers and AppSec engineers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Prototype pollution converts untrusted object merges into global behavioral changes. In large Node.js applications, that can corrupt authorization checks, logging behavior, template rendering, and security middleware assumptions. Exploitability is determined by gadget availability, so teams need both source controls and runtime hardening.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authorization decisions, request metadata, template and serialization flows
- Entry points: deep merge helpers, querystring parsers, JSON patch endpoints
- Trust boundaries: user input to object composition and framework internals
- Typical attacker goals: privilege bypass, stored persistence, denial of service

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers inject special keys like __proto__, constructor, or prototype through nested structures. If parsing and merge utilities do not block dangerous keys, polluted properties propagate into application logic. Attackers then trigger gadgets that treat polluted values as trusted configuration flags.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: locate all merge and clone operations over untrusted structures
- Dynamic checks: nested payload fuzzing and gadget-trigger test suites
- Telemetry indicators: anomalous default values and policy drift in runtime logs
- False positive control: demonstrate controlled property pollution and downstream effect

## Secure Implementation
Reject dangerous property names before parsing, use hardened merge utilities, and freeze configuration objects where possible. Enforce schema validation for nested inputs, and add runtime assertions for security-critical flags.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Block __proto__, constructor, and prototype keys
- [x] Replace unsafe deep-merge dependencies
- [x] Apply schema validation to nested objects
- [x] Add exploit-oriented regression tests for known gadgets
- [x] Monitor for unexpected config mutation at runtime

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: injection-adjacent integrity risks
- CWE: CWE-1321, CWE-915
- WSTG: input validation and server-side logic testing
- ASVS: input processing and secure coding controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Prototype_Pollution_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/prototype-pollution
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1321.html
author: Security Team

title: Business Logic Abuse in Checkout and Subscription Workflows
excerpt: A specialist method for modeling and preventing workflow abuse in pricing, discount, and subscription state transitions.
content:
# Business Logic Abuse in Checkout and Subscription Workflows

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: product engineers, QA security analysts, fraud defense teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Business logic vulnerabilities bypass technical controls because the application behaves as implemented, but not as intended. Attackers exploit ordering assumptions, race windows, and weak state constraints to get unauthorized discounts, free upgrades, or irreversible account state changes.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: revenue, plan entitlements, billing integrity, account lifecycle data
- Entry points: checkout APIs, coupon validation flows, plan upgrade endpoints
- Trust boundaries: frontend workflow state to backend authoritative state machine
- Typical attacker goals: free service access, discount abuse, fraud at scale

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers replay and reorder API calls, combine stale cart snapshots with new promotions, and exploit asynchronous validation delays. They probe transitions that were designed for support operators but accidentally exposed to user-level paths. They chain this with race conditions to apply conflicting states before reconciliation jobs run.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: explicit state-machine definition and forbidden transitions
- Dynamic checks: multi-step sequence mutation and parallel request race testing
- Telemetry indicators: negative-margin transactions and unusual plan transition patterns
- False positive control: replay exact call sequence in isolated environment and verify impact

## Secure Implementation
Build server-side state machines with strict preconditions and idempotency keys. Validate all monetary and entitlement outcomes against authoritative backend state, not client-submitted values. Enforce atomic transactions for multi-step billing operations and include post-condition checks.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Server authoritative state transitions only
- [x] Idempotency and replay prevention for payment operations
- [x] Atomic checkout and entitlement updates
- [x] Fraud analytics and anomaly controls on promotion usage
- [x] Security tests for forbidden workflow transitions

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: insecure design and business logic weakness themes
- CWE: CWE-840, CWE-841
- WSTG: business logic testing methodology
- ASVS: architectural controls and integrity validation

## References
1. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Abuse_Case_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/840.html
author: Security Team

title: Race Conditions in Financial APIs: Consistency, Idempotency, and Lock Strategy
excerpt: Engineering-focused defense of financial APIs against race-driven double spend and inconsistent balance states.
content:
# Race Conditions in Financial APIs: Consistency, Idempotency, and Lock Strategy

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend engineers and platform reliability teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Race conditions are not only availability bugs; they are often exploitable integrity flaws. In payment and wallet systems, concurrent requests can trigger double credits, duplicate refunds, or bypassed spending limits when consistency assumptions are broken.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: account balances, transfer integrity, settlement records
- Entry points: transfer, refund, withdrawal, and webhook reconciliation endpoints
- Trust boundaries: API request layer to transactional datastore
- Typical attacker goals: double spend, balance inflation, settlement mismatch

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers send parallel requests that target the same resource and exploit check-then-act logic. They combine retries, timeout-induced replays, and multi-node load balancing to hit weak transaction boundaries. They may also manipulate webhook ordering to produce stale state acceptance.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: identify non-atomic decision points in money movement code
- Dynamic checks: high-concurrency replay with controlled idempotency keys
- Telemetry indicators: duplicate transaction IDs, reconciliation drift, negative reserve anomalies
- False positive control: reconstruct event sequence and storage commit order

## Secure Implementation
Use idempotency keys enforced by durable unique constraints, apply transactional row-level locks for critical updates, and validate post-conditions after commit. Design event consumers for ordered or version-aware processing and reject stale updates.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Durable idempotency guarantees per operation class
- [x] Atomic update paths for balance-critical logic
- [x] Consistency checks at write and reconciliation time
- [x] Retry-safe webhook and async processing design
- [x] Concurrency abuse tests in CI and staging

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: business logic and integrity risk areas
- CWE: CWE-362, CWE-367
- WSTG: workflow and transaction abuse testing
- ASVS: transaction integrity and concurrency safeguards

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/race-conditions
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/362.html
author: Security Team
